title: Number of coral bleaching events by stage of the ENSO cycle
subtitle: [Coral bleaching](#dod:coral_bleaching) typically occurs when water temperatures rise above the normal range for the coral's habitat.  This is more likely during El Niño stages of the [ENSO cycle](#dod:enso_cycle) when tropical sea temperatures are warmer.
caption:
Number of coral bleaching events by stage of the ENSO cycle
Coral bleaching typically occurs when water temperatures rise above the normal range for the coral's habitat. This is more likely during El Niño stages of the ENSO cycle when tropical sea temperatures are warmer.

The bar chart tracks the annual count of coral bleaching events from 1980 through 2016, categorized by the phase of the El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO) cycle. The vertical axis measures the number of events, ranging from zero to over 70, while the horizontal axis marks specific years. A legend distinguishes three phases: El Niño represented by dark red bars, Moderate phases by purple bars, and La Niña by blue bars. The data shows a concentration of high-magnitude events during El Niño years, with the highest single bar appearing in 1998, where the count exceeds 70 events. Another significant peak for El Niño occurs in 2016, reaching just over 50 events, followed by a value near 30 in 2015. Moderate phases show variable activity, with a notable cluster between 2001 and 2006 where counts hover between 15 and 40 events. La Niña years generally display lower frequencies, though a distinct spike occurs in 2010 with a count surpassing 50 events. Earlier years in the timeline, such as 1980 and 1987, show El Niño events ranging between 20 and 25, while the intervening years often feature lower counts or Moderate phase activity. The visual pattern places the largest bars within the El Niño category, particularly in the late 1990s and mid-2010s, while La Niña bars remain shorter for most of the recorded period except for the 2010 anomaly.
